questions.q: What does a spectrogram show that a static Fourier spectrum does not?
options: The total energy of the signal
How frequency content changes over time, displayed as intensity in a time-frequency plot
The phase relationship between harmonics
The sampling rate used for the signal
questions.explanation: A spectrogram computes the Fourier transform of short overlapping signal segments (using the Short-Time Fourier Transform), revealing which frequencies are present at which times. A static spectrum shows only the overall frequency content averaged over the entire signal.

questions.q: In audio processing, what is the purpose of windowing?
options: To increase the sampling rate of the signal
To reduce spectral leakage by tapering the edges of analysis frames before FFT
To eliminate all frequency components above 20 kHz
To compress the audio for storage
questions.explanation: Windowing applies a taper function (Hann, Hamming, Blackman) that goes to zero at frame edges, reducing discontinuities. This attenuates the sinc-function side lobes of spectral leakage. Rectangular (no) windowing produces the most leakage.

questions.q: What are MFCCs (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) primarily used for in audio processing?
options: Displaying high-quality audio waveforms on a screen
Feature extraction for speech recognition and audio classification, modeling human auditory perception
Removing background noise from audio recordings
Increasing the bit depth of audio samples
questions.explanation: MFCCs compress the frequency spectrum into mel-scale coefficients that mirror human hearing: we perceive frequency logarithmically and are more sensitive to lower frequencies. They're widely used in voice recognition, speaker identification, and music information retrieval.

questions.q: What is the mel scale in the context of audio processing?
options: A unit measuring the amplitude of sound in decibels
A nonlinear frequency scale that matches human perception of pitch, where equal mel intervals correspond to equal perceived intervals
The rate at which the sampling frequency must be divided
A measure of compression ratio in audio codecs
questions.explanation: The mel scale is perceptually motivated: humans perceive frequency logarithmically. The formula mel = 2595 × log10(1 + f/700) maps Hz to mels. At low frequencies, mels ≈ f; at high frequencies, mels ≈ 2595 log10(f).

questions.q: In audio equalization, what does boosting a frequency band at 2 kHz do to the perceived sound?
options: Makes the audio sound lower in pitch
Increases the perceived brightness and presence; emphasizes consonants and attack transients
Reduces the frequency content above 2 kHz proportionally
Eliminates aliasing in the digital audio
questions.explanation: The 2 kHz region is critical for clarity and presence in audio. Boosting it enhances definition, especially for speech (consonants) and instrument attacks. The midrange (1-4 kHz) is perceptually important; adjusting it noticeably changes audio character.

questions.q: What is the purpose of overlap-add in Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) processing?
options: To increase the FFT length for better frequency resolution
To reconstruct a continuous signal from overlapping windowed frames without artifacts
To reduce the number of computations required
To eliminate the phase information from the spectrum
questions.explanation: STFT divides the signal into overlapping frames (typically 50% overlap). After processing each frame independently, the modified frames are reconstructed by overlap-add (summing overlapping portions). With proper window design, this reconstruction is perfect.

questions.q: In audio compression (codecs like MP3), what is the purpose of perceptual coding?
options: To amplify quiet parts of the audio
To remove frequency components humans cannot hear or notice due to masking, reducing file size
To increase the bit depth of audio samples
To eliminate echo and reverberation
questions.explanation: Perceptual coding exploits psychoacoustics: if a loud tone masks a quiet tone nearby, the quiet one can be removed without audible quality loss. This reduces data dramatically (MP3 achieves 10:1 compression) while remaining transparent to human listeners.

questions.q: What is spectral masking in the context of audio perception?
options: A filtering technique to remove background noise
The phenomenon where a loud sound makes nearby frequencies inaudible due to auditory system limitations
A method to increase the perceived loudness of audio
The process of converting audio to the frequency domain
questions.explanation: Spectral masking occurs when a strong frequency component suppresses the perception of weaker components in nearby frequencies. The auditory system has limited frequency resolution; a loud tone 'masks' quieter tones within its critical band, making them inaudible.

questions.q: What is the relationship between window length and frequency resolution in STFT?
options: Longer windows provide better frequency resolution but worse time resolution
Longer windows provide better time resolution but worse frequency resolution
Window length has no effect on resolution
Longer windows provide better resolution in both time and frequency
questions.explanation: Frequency resolution depends on window length: Δf ≈ fs/N, where N is window length. Longer windows have narrower frequency bins (better resolution). But longer windows integrate over more time, reducing time localization (worse temporal resolution).

questions.q: What is temporal masking in audio processing?
options: Removing low-frequency components from the signal
The inability to perceive a weak sound shortly before or after a loud sound, due to auditory system dynamics
The phase distortion introduced by audio filters
Compressing the dynamic range of an audio signal
questions.explanation: Temporal masking has pre-masking (backward masking, a few ms before) and post-masking (forward masking, tens of ms after). A loud sound temporarily raises the hearing threshold, making quieter sounds inaudible if they occur nearby in time. Exploiting this is key to perceptual audio coding.
